comment_text.sn12.23:1.1: This discourse uniquely links the conditional arising of suffering with the conditional unfolding of the path, showing that liberation is not possible without suffering.
comment_text.sn12.23:1.2: The “ending of defilements” (āsavānaṁ khayaṁ) is a common sutta term for arahantship. | “Knowing and seeing” refers to the penetrating insight that arises from meditative immersion (samādhi, eg. AN 5.168:2.2, AN 10.2:2.3, SN 12.23:6.18, DN 2:83.1). | The opening is shared with SN 22.101, SN 56.25, MN 2, and Iti 102.
comment_text.sn12.23:2.1: An upanisā is a conditional factor that is both necessary and strongly conducive. It functions as a contraction of upanissaya (“rely on”). Elsewhere, saupanisa is used of the seven path factors as “vital conditions” for right immersion (SN 45.28:1.2, MN 117:2.1). Pali upanisā is also found in the distinct sense “sitting close” to a teacher (AN 3.67:6.7). | It is not clear if upanisā is the same as the Sanskrit upaniṣad, for which the oldest sense is “correspondence, equivalence”, referring to the secret links between things (Śatapatha Brāhmaṇa 10.4.5.1; Bṛhadāraṇyaka Upaniṣad 3.9.26, 4.2.1; Chāndogya Upaniṣad 3.11.3, 8.8.4; Taittirīya Upaniṣad 1.11.4, 2.9.1). These equivalences are explained in the esoteric teachings that themselves came to be known as Upaniṣads.
comment_text.sn12.23:2.3: “Freedom” here is the liberation from defilements, namely arahantship itself. The arahant reviews their freedom, which is the “knowledge of ending”.
comment_text.sn12.23:2.6: “Dispassion” (virāga) has a dual sense in Pali: a state where lust (rāga) is absent, and the process of defilements “fading away”, which leans on the sense of rāga as “dye” (eg. the “fading away of ignorance” at SN 12.51:9.4).
comment_text.sn12.23:2.9: “Disillusionment” (nibbidā) is the turning away in disappointment upon realizing that one cannot get what one wants (MN 50:31.15, SN 35.240:1.8).
comment_text.sn12.23:2.12: This is the insight of stream-entry.
comment_text.sn12.23:2.15: “Immersion” (samādhi) is a general term for the absorptions (jhāna) and other profound meditations free of the hindrances. It is an integrated emotional state of elevated consciousness, during which the mind is extremely bright and powerful. For a meditator with right view, the empowered mind emerging from immersion naturally inclines towards insight into the suffering of holding on and the bliss of letting go.
comment_text.sn12.23:3.2: Because immersion is an emotional release, its primary condition is deep, pervasive, lasting, and subtle bliss or pleasure (sukha). It cannot be realized with purely neutral feeling.
comment_text.sn12.23:3.5: “Tranquility” is a settled and serene body and mind, as activities or energies become stilled through the process of meditation.
comment_text.sn12.23:3.8: “Rapture” (pīti) is a heightened emotional response to happiness or joy. Minor forms appear as tingling or thrill, and it deepens into a pervasive spiritual sense of elevation or uplift in meditation.
comment_text.sn12.23:3.11: “Joy” (pāmojja) is the upwelling of happiness connected with wholesome things, such as hearing the Dhamma (MN 33:23.2), or reflecting on one’s progress (MN 7:11.1, MN 40:8.2) or ethical purity (AN 10.2:1.6).
comment_text.sn12.23:3.14: “Faith” inspires the courage to progress. It is a “grounded faith” based on experience, and tested through the path (MN 47:16.1).
comment_text.sn12.23:4.2: Someone afflicted by suffering looks about in despair to find anyone who can help. When they hear the Buddha or a disciple speaking about suffering, faith arises (AN 6.63:42.3). | With this link, the discourse reveals the connection between the path to freedom and dependent origination. Suffering is the condition into which we have been born, but the experience of suffering is also the stimulus we need to seek freedom.
comment_text.sn12.23:4.5: From here the discourse proceeds back through the standard links in reverse order.
comment_text.sn12.23:6.1: The syntax is subtly different to the standard sequence (SN 12.2:2.2). There, the initial term is ablative, which conveys the causal sense and might be literally rendered, “Due to the ignorance dependency, choices”. Here the two terms are both nominative, so there is no causal sense in the nouns. We must assume, then, that the causal sense is supplied by the implicit verb hoti in the pregnant sense “bring about, beget, yield”. A literal rendering might be, “The ignorance vital condition begets choices.”
comment_text.sn12.23:7.1: This simile is also found in SN 55.38, AN 3.95, AN 4.147, AN 10.61, and AN 10.62. Compare Kaṭha Upaniṣad 4.15.

root_text.sn12.23:1.2: “Jānato ahaṁ, bhikkhave, passato āsavānaṁ khayaṁ vadāmi, no ajānato no apassato.
root_text.sn12.23:1.3: Kiñca, bhikkhave, jānato kiṁ passato āsavānaṁ khayo hoti?
root_text.sn12.23:1.4: Iti rūpaṁ iti rūpassa samudayo iti rūpassa atthaṅgamo,
root_text.sn12.23:1.8: iti viññāṇaṁ iti viññāṇassa samudayo iti viññāṇassa atthaṅgamoti.
root_text.sn12.23:1.9: Evaṁ kho, bhikkhave, jānato evaṁ passato āsavānaṁ khayo hoti.
root_text.sn12.23:2.1: Yampissa taṁ, bhikkhave, khayasmiṁ khaye ñāṇaṁ, tampi saupanisaṁ vadāmi, no anupanisaṁ.
root_text.sn12.23:2.2: Kā ca, bhikkhave, khaye ñāṇassa upanisā?
root_text.sn12.23:2.4: Vimuttimpāhaṁ, bhikkhave, saupanisaṁ vadāmi, no anupanisaṁ.
root_text.sn12.23:2.7: Virāgampāhaṁ, bhikkhave, saupanisaṁ vadāmi, no anupanisaṁ.
root_text.sn12.23:2.10: Nibbidampāhaṁ, bhikkhave, saupanisaṁ vadāmi, no anupanisaṁ.
root_text.sn12.23:2.13: Yathābhūtañāṇadassanampāhaṁ, bhikkhave, saupanisaṁ vadāmi, no anupanisaṁ.
root_text.sn12.23:2.16: Samādhimpāhaṁ, bhikkhave, saupanisaṁ vadāmi, no anupanisaṁ.
root_text.sn12.23:3.3: Sukhampāhaṁ, bhikkhave, saupanisaṁ vadāmi, no anupanisaṁ.
root_text.sn12.23:3.6: Passaddhimpāhaṁ, bhikkhave, saupanisaṁ vadāmi, no anupanisaṁ.
root_text.sn12.23:3.9: Pītimpāhaṁ, bhikkhave, saupanisaṁ vadāmi, no anupanisaṁ.
root_text.sn12.23:3.12: Pāmojjampāhaṁ, bhikkhave, saupanisaṁ vadāmi, no anupanisaṁ.
root_text.sn12.23:3.15: Saddhampāhaṁ, bhikkhave, saupanisaṁ vadāmi, no anupanisaṁ.
root_text.sn12.23:4.3: Dukkhampāhaṁ, bhikkhave, saupanisaṁ vadāmi, no anupanisaṁ.
root_text.sn12.23:4.6: Jātimpāhaṁ, bhikkhave, saupanisaṁ vadāmi, no anupanisaṁ.
root_text.sn12.23:4.9: Bhavampāhaṁ, bhikkhave, saupanisaṁ vadāmi, no anupanisaṁ.
root_text.sn12.23:4.12: Upādānampāhaṁ, bhikkhave, saupanisaṁ vadāmi, no anupanisaṁ.
root_text.sn12.23:4.15: Taṇhampāhaṁ, bhikkhave, saupanisaṁ vadāmi, no anupanisaṁ.
root_text.sn12.23:5.8: Saṅkhārepāhaṁ, bhikkhave, saupanise vadāmi, no anupanise.
root_text.sn12.23:7.1: Seyyathāpi, bhikkhave, uparipabbate thullaphusitake deve vassante taṁ udakaṁ yathāninnaṁ pavattamānaṁ pabbatakandarapadarasākhā paripūreti. Pabbatakandarapadarasākhāparipūrā kusobbhe paripūrenti. Kusobbhā paripūrā mahāsobbhe paripūrenti. Mahāsobbhā paripūrā kunnadiyo paripūrenti. Kunnadiyo paripūrā mahānadiyo paripūrenti. Mahānadiyo paripūrā mahāsamuddaṁ paripūrenti.
root_text.sn12.23:8.1: Evameva kho, bhikkhave, avijjūpanisā saṅkhārā, saṅkhārūpanisaṁ viññāṇaṁ, viññāṇūpanisaṁ nāmarūpaṁ, nāmarūpūpanisaṁ saḷāyatanaṁ, saḷāyatanūpaniso phasso, phassūpanisā vedanā, vedanūpanisā taṇhā, taṇhūpanisaṁ upādānaṁ, upādānūpaniso bhavo, bhavūpanisā jāti, jātūpanisaṁ dukkhaṁ, dukkhūpanisā saddhā, saddhūpanisaṁ pāmojjaṁ, pāmojjūpanisā pīti, pītūpanisā passaddhi, passaddhūpanisaṁ sukhaṁ, sukhūpaniso samādhi, samādhūpanisaṁ yathābhūtañāṇadassanaṁ, yathābhūtañāṇadassanūpanisā nibbidā, nibbidūpaniso virāgo, virāgūpanisā vimutti, vimuttūpanisaṁ khaye ñāṇan”ti.

translation_text.sn12.23:1.2: “Mendicants, I say that the ending of defilements is for one who knows and sees, not for one who does not know or see.
translation_text.sn12.23:1.3: For one who knows and sees what?
translation_text.sn12.23:1.4: ‘Such is form, such is the origin of form, such is the ending of form.
translation_text.sn12.23:1.8: Such is consciousness, such is the origin of consciousness, such is the ending of consciousness.’
translation_text.sn12.23:1.9: The ending of the defilements is for one who knows and sees this.
translation_text.sn12.23:2.1: I say that this knowledge of ending has a vital condition, it doesn’t lack a vital condition.
translation_text.sn12.23:2.4: I say that freedom has a vital condition, it doesn’t lack a vital condition.
translation_text.sn12.23:2.7: I say that dispassion has a vital condition.
translation_text.sn12.23:2.10: I say that disillusionment has a vital condition.
translation_text.sn12.23:2.12: You should say: ‘Truly knowing and seeing.’
translation_text.sn12.23:2.13: I say that truly knowing and seeing has a vital condition.
translation_text.sn12.23:2.16: I say that immersion has a vital condition.
translation_text.sn12.23:3.3: I say that bliss has a vital condition.
translation_text.sn12.23:3.6: I say that tranquility has a vital condition.
translation_text.sn12.23:3.9: I say that rapture has a vital condition.
translation_text.sn12.23:3.12: I say that joy has a vital condition.
translation_text.sn12.23:3.15: I say that faith has a vital condition.
translation_text.sn12.23:4.3: I say that suffering has a vital condition.
translation_text.sn12.23:4.6: I say that rebirth has a vital condition.
translation_text.sn12.23:4.9: I say that continued existence has a vital condition.
translation_text.sn12.23:4.12: I say that grasping has a vital condition.
translation_text.sn12.23:4.15: I say that craving has a vital condition.
translation_text.sn12.23:5.4: You should say: ‘The six sense fields.’ …
translation_text.sn12.23:5.8: I say that choices have a vital condition, they don’t lack a vital condition.
translation_text.sn12.23:5.9: And what is the vital condition for choices?
translation_text.sn12.23:6.1: So, mendicants: ignorance is the vital condition for choices.
translation_text.sn12.23:6.2: Choices are the vital condition for consciousness.
translation_text.sn12.23:6.3: Consciousness is the vital condition for name and form.
translation_text.sn12.23:6.4: Name and form are the vital conditions for the six sense fields.
translation_text.sn12.23:6.5: The six sense fields are the vital conditions for contact.
translation_text.sn12.23:6.6: Contact is the vital condition for feeling.
translation_text.sn12.23:6.7: Feeling is the vital condition for craving.
translation_text.sn12.23:6.8: Craving is the vital condition for grasping.
translation_text.sn12.23:6.9: Grasping is the vital condition for continued existence.
translation_text.sn12.23:6.10: Continued existence is the vital condition for rebirth.
translation_text.sn12.23:6.11: Rebirth is the vital condition for suffering.
translation_text.sn12.23:6.12: Suffering is the vital condition for faith.
translation_text.sn12.23:6.13: Faith is the vital condition for joy.
translation_text.sn12.23:6.14: Joy is the vital condition for rapture.
translation_text.sn12.23:6.15: Rapture is the vital condition for tranquility.
translation_text.sn12.23:6.16: Tranquility is the vital condition for bliss.
translation_text.sn12.23:6.17: Bliss is the vital condition for immersion.
translation_text.sn12.23:6.18: Immersion is the vital condition for truly knowing and seeing.
translation_text.sn12.23:6.19: Truly knowing and seeing is the vital condition for disillusionment.
translation_text.sn12.23:6.20: Disillusionment is the vital condition for dispassion.
translation_text.sn12.23:6.21: Dispassion is the vital condition for freedom.
translation_text.sn12.23:6.22: Freedom is the vital condition for the knowledge of ending.
translation_text.sn12.23:7.1: It’s like when the heavens rain heavily on a mountain top, and the water flows downhill to fill the hollows, crevices, and creeks. As they become full, they fill up the pools. The pools fill up the lakes, the lakes fill up the streams, and the streams fill up the rivers. And as the rivers become full, they fill up the ocean.
translation_text.sn12.23:8.1: In the same way, ignorance is the vital condition for choices. … Freedom is the vital condition for the knowledge of ending.”

variant_text.sn12.23:7.1: kusobbhe → kussubbhe (bj, sya-all, km); kusubbhe (pts1ed, pts2ed)
